How Artificial Intelligence is Revolutionizing HR, Sales and Marketing: From Old School Techniques to Intelligent Business Approaches

10 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A decade back, getting the perfect employee, selling and making a successful marketing strategy involved much more human effort, experience and intuition, and many hours of manual labor. The recruiters had to spend days reading resumes, sales representatives had to conduct cold calling, and marketers used to invest a lot into the campaign without even knowing whether it would reach the target audience. This situation has changed completely.
Artificial intelligence is among the largest factors contributing to business evolution today. AI is not the technology available only to giant tech firms and movies anymore. Companies utilize AI technologies to automate the process of routine tasks, analyze huge amounts of information, and make decisions much faster and more intelligent.
And the interesting thing about this is that AI does not replace people working in HR, Sales and Marketing departments. It changes their way of doing things. The recruiters have more time to establish relationships than reading the resumes. The sales representatives talk to the customers meaningfully instead of working with Excel sheets. And marketers know everything about their preferences.
AI is not only an advancement in technology, but it is also an evolution in business thinking from creativity to prediction and analytics. Businesses that used to make decisions based on instinct alone have learned to harness the power of machines for productivity, customer satisfaction, and success in the increasingly complex marketplace.
Such evolution is seen especially well in HR, Sales, and Marketing — business domains that deal extensively with information, communication, and decision making. Studying how AI changed those domains will show you why it has turned out to be such a valuable tool in the world of business today.
How Business Operated Before the emergence of AI
In order to comprehend how AI is helping organizations today, it is important to first take a look at how businesses operated before intelligent automation gained popularity.
Human Resource Departments had a very time-consuming task of hiring new employees. Hiring processes involved manually going through hundreds of resumes, setting up numerous interviews via emails, and having the final selection depending solely on personal choice of the recruiter. Although there was a great deal of experience required in the process, it was inefficient and subject to subconscious biases. On top of that, much time of HR workers was taken by administrative tasks like onboarding, attendance control, salaries management, and employees records keeping.
Sales departments were not exempt from similar inefficiency. The customer information was scattered among different sources such as spreadsheets, note pads, and databases. It would then take hours to search through them all and find potential leads, make cold calls and follow-up on them manually. The forecast of future sales was done mostly on the basis of previous experience and guesswork.
Of all the departments, marketing was most prone to making sweeping assumptions concerning consumer behavior. Marketing campaigns were geared towards masses in the hope that some percent of the masses would act. TV advertisements, newspaper ads, and billboards targeted many people; however, it was difficult to tell how many of those people responded. Measuring the effectiveness of the campaign was a lengthy process.
This approach may have been quite effective, resulting in the creation of successful businesses for years. Nevertheless, one feature of this approach united all companies using it: decision-making was manual-based and depended on historical data. As the scale of businesses grew and customers’ expectations changed, these traditional ways became less feasible.
The rapid development of technology resulted in an enormous amount of data. The more business processes were conducted using the Internet, the more valuable information was collected. However, analyzing such a huge amount of data manually seemed to be an impossible task. And that is when AI made its debut and turned everything upside down.
Firstly, with the emergence of Artificial Intelligence, businesses received the possibility not only to automate routine operations but also to learn from the data, detect trends, and make predictions that help to make better decisions. Thus, there was no need to wait until the end of a quarter to analyze the results. With AI, it became possible to obtain all necessary data in real time and take appropriate measures to respond to any changes.
For example, instead of going through each resume one by one, recruiters got the opportunity to detect those applicants who were perfectly matched for a certain position. Sales managers could predict which leads would become customers and focus on them. Marketers could instantly detect customer preferences and make personalized recommendations rather than just advertise their products.
This does not mean that AI reduced the necessity of human skills. What it actually did was provide room for humans to use their time solving issues rather than doing repetitive tasks.
AI Transforms Human Resource Management
In the context of the evolution of different departments within businesses , the emergence of AI in HR department makes Recruitment, employee engagement, performance management, and workforce planning faster, more efficient, and more data-driven.
From Resumes Sorting to Intelligent Matching
It can be said that the traditional approach to recruitment used to be called looking for a needle in a haystack. Hundreds or even thousands of resumes had to be analyzed manually by HR specialists in order to select the right candidate for the position.
Apart from wasting considerable amounts of time, such an approach increased the possibility of missing out good candidates who might have been overlooked.
However, now recruitment is powered by intelligent software that analyzes not only keywords but skills, qualifications, career history, and even transferable abilities of each candidate.
AI does not make decisions about whom to hire but gives the recruiter a shortlist of suitable candidates in order to interview them and see whether they fit the position and organization culturally and from the long-term perspective. One of such companies that have implemented AI in recruitment is Unilever.
Employee Onboarding and Learning
The use of AI does not stop at just recruitment. After a candidate joins an organization, the process of onboarding entails the completion of several forms, training sessions, policy briefs, and various administrative tasks. Conventionally, this was a very tedious process entailing lots of coordination among the HR department, managers, and the newly hired employees.
Today, AI has made this process much easier through virtual assistants and custom learning portals. The new employees get customized training suggestions, access policies instantly, fill in any required forms electronically, and even find answers to commonly asked questions. In this way, the HR departments save time from paperwork and concentrate on making engaging experiences for the employees.
Performance Management
Evaluations of individual performance were done through annual appraisals, which consisted of assessments of the individual’s performance conducted by the manager based on his observations in the preceding year. Though helpful, this system often failed to take into account the employee’s continuous efforts and provided feedback much later.
The introduction of AI has enabled continuous performance management through analysis of project results, productivity rates, employee feedback, learning process, and collaboration process. Managers receive useful information that helps them see the accomplishments, areas for improvement, and continuously work with their staff, not only during a certain cycle of appraisal.
It should be noted that this information is meant to be used to spark conversation, not as an alternative to it. Human judgment is necessary when assessing performance and identifying its potential.
How AI is Revolutionizing the Process of Sales
For many years, a successful sale was mainly driven by perseverance, building relationships, and experience. Sales people used to spend many hours searching for new clients, making
cold calls, filling customer databases, and doing follow-ups manually. Although such techniques usually helped to achieve their goal, they were very consuming in terms of time and left no place for strategy.AI technologies have completely revolutionized this process. Rather than being a substitute for salespeople, AI became a perfect decision support tool for them.
From Cold Calling to Intelligently Picking Out Leads
One of the major problems associated with traditional sales approaches is that it was very difficult to recognize whether or not a prospect would actually show interest in buying the product or the services being offered by the company.
Predictive lead scoring using artificial intelligence technology enables companies to analyze the behavior, web interactions, past purchases, emails and demographics of the prospects. The system then picks out leads that have the greatest chances of becoming customers.
Salespeople no longer needed to do hundreds of cold calls like before instead, they will be able to communicate with prospects who are already showing a lot of interest in buying something by analyzing previous data.
Improved Sales Forecasting
With the introduction of AI, there is now an improved way of doing sales forecasting. This is done through constant analysis of sales data in conjunction with the current market trends, customer relations, and seasonal trends. This gives more accurate predictions which help in budgeting, planning, and allocating resources. It enables companies to take proactive action rather than acting reactively.
Improving Customer Relations
In today’s world, consumers expect companies to know their needs. General conversations are no longer useful since customers appreciate the relevancy of personalized service.
AI-driven CRM solutions help sales representatives know consumer preferences by collecting data from different sources. Previous transactions, online activity, questions posed by customers, and all kinds of communications are analyzed, providing salespeople with a full understanding of each client. Instead of asking a general question such as “How can I assist you today?”, sales representatives may start the dialogue with recommendations tailored to clients’ preferences.
Salesforce is one company that uses AI within its CRM platform thanks to such solutions as Einstein AI. With this technology, sales organizations can prioritize leads, automate the administrative process, and gain valuable insights on their clients. The example shows that AI does not replace salespeople but rather helps them become more knowledgeable and productive.
AI and Marketing
If Human Resources revolves around personnel while Sales revolves around money, then Marketing connects the two by creating awareness, instilling trust, and driving demand. Within the last decade, AI can be said to have made most headway in marketing since it is largely centered on customer behavior.
From Mass Marketing to Personalization
The conventional marketing strategy relied on a one-size-fits-all model. It meant that a commercial aired on television, advertised in a newspaper, or put up on a poster was seen by everyone irrespective of whether they were interested in the product or had been buying it previously or not.
